message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We chronicle SARS-CoV-2 sero-prevalence through eight cross-sectional sero-surveys (snapshots) in the Lower Mainland (Greater Vancouver and Fraser Valley), British Columbia, Canada from March 2020 to August 2022.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Anonymized-residual sera were obtained from children and adults attending an outpatient laboratory network. Sera were tested with at least three immuno-assays per snapshot to detect spike (S1) and/or nucleocapsid protein (NP) antibodies. Sero-prevalence was defined by dual-assay positivity, including any or infection-induced, the latter requiring S1+NP antibody detection from January 2021 owing to vaccine availability. Infection-induced estimates were used to assess the extent to which surveillance case reports under-estimated infections.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Sero-prevalence was ≤1% by the 3
                    <jats:sup>rd</jats:sup>
                    snapshot in September 2020 and <5% by January 2021 (4
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    ). Following vaccine roll-out, sero-prevalence increased to >55% by May/June 2021 (5
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    ), ∼80% by September/October 2021 (6
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    ), and >95% by March 2022 (7
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    ). In all age groups, infection-induced sero-prevalence remained <15% through September/October 2021, increasing through subsequent Omicron waves to ∼40% by March 2022 (7
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    ) and ∼60% by July/August 2022 (8
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    ). By August 2022, at least 70-80% of children ≤19 years, 60-70% of adults 20-59 years, but ∼40% of adults ≥60 years had been infected. Surveillance case reports under-estimated infections by 12-fold between the 6
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    -7
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    and 92-fold between the 7
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    -8
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    snapshots.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Interpretation</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>By August 2022, most children and adults had acquired SARS-CoV-2 vaccine and infection exposures, resulting in more robust hybrid immunity. Conversely the elderly, still at greatest risk of severe outcomes, remain largely-dependent on vaccine-induced protection alone, and should be prioritized for additional doses.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Serial cross-sectional estimation of vaccine and infection-induced SARS-CoV-2 sero-prevalence in children and adults, British Columbia, Canada: March 2020 to August 2022
